structured.summary: This paper proposes Contextualized Generative Retrieval (CGR), a novel generative retrieval model that leverages contextualized token embeddings as decoder vocab embeddings to combine the benefits of parametric and non-parametric information for document retrieval. The approach outperforms existing generative retrieval models on multiple benchmarks by effectively utilizing contextualized embeddings and contrastive learning.

strengths.point: Innovative combination of parametric and non-parametric spaces in generative retrieval.
strengths.evidence: Section 3 describes how CGR uses contextualized embeddings as decoder vocab embeddings, enabling interaction in both parametric and non-parametric spaces.

strengths.point: Significant empirical performance improvements over strong baselines.
strengths.evidence: Section 5.2 reports 6% average improvement on KILT datasets and over 2x improvement on NQ-320k compared to GENRE and DSI.

strengths.point: Comprehensive ablation studies and analyses to understand model components.
strengths.evidence: Sections 5.2 and Appendix C provide detailed experiments comparing CGR variants, effects of contrastive learning, clustering, and contextualized embeddings.

strengths.point: Effective use of contrastive learning to improve embedding space quality.
strengths.evidence: Section 4.3 and experimental results in Section 5.2 show CGR Contra variant achieves the best performance by first training with contrastive loss.

weaknesses.point: High computational and storage cost due to large contextualized embedding matrices.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4.4 discusses the need for clustering to reduce storage from 148GB to manageable sizes, indicating scalability concerns.

weaknesses.point: Dependence on pre-extracted contextualized embeddings may limit adaptability during training.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4.1 mentions EMB is frozen in CGR Base, and asynchronous updates in CGR Async add complexity.

weaknesses.point: Limited evaluation on full KILT dataset due to resource constraints.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 5.1 notes CGR was trained on subsets of KILT, and full dataset training was not performed unlike GENRE.

weaknesses.point: Potential complexity in managing multiple token IDs per token in decoder vocabulary.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 3 explains that multiple contextualized embeddings per token require assigning multiple token IDs, which may complicate decoding.

weaknesses.point: Lack of discussion on inference latency and efficiency compared to bi-encoder models.
weaknesses.evidence: While storage footprint is discussed, the paper does not provide detailed analysis of inference speed or computational overhead.

weaknesses.point: Unclear how well the approach generalizes to other retrieval tasks beyond document title retrieval.
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments focus on document retrieval with titles as targets; applicability to other retrieval formats is not explored.

weaknesses.point: The method relies on constrained beam search with prefix trees, which may limit flexibility.
weaknesses.evidence: Appendix A.1 describes constrained beam search to ensure generated sequences are in corpus, potentially restricting generation diversity.

questions.question: How does CGR perform in terms of inference latency and computational cost compared to bi-encoder and vanilla generative retrieval models?
questions.motivation: Understanding efficiency is critical for practical deployment of retrieval systems.

questions.question: Can the approach be extended to retrieval tasks where targets are not fixed sequences like titles, e.g., passage retrieval or open-domain QA?
questions.motivation: Evaluating generalizability would broaden the impact of the method.

questions.question: How sensitive is the model performance to the choice of clustering parameter k and frequency of EMB updates in CGR Async?
questions.motivation: Clarifying hyperparameter effects would help practitioners optimize the model.

questions.question: Is it possible to jointly train EMB and RET end-to-end to improve coherency without incurring prohibitive computational costs?
questions.motivation: Joint training might enhance embedding alignment and retrieval accuracy.

critique_points.point_001: High computational and storage cost due to large contextualized embedding matrices. (Section 4.4 discusses the need for clustering to reduce storage from 148GB to manageable sizes, indicating scalability concerns.)
critique_points.point_002: Dependence on pre-extracted contextualized embeddings may limit adaptability during training. (Section 4.1 mentions EMB is frozen in CGR Base, and asynchronous updates in CGR Async add complexity.)
critique_points.point_003: Limited evaluation on full KILT dataset due to resource constraints. (Section 5.1 notes CGR was trained on subsets of KILT, and full dataset training was not performed unlike GENRE.)
critique_points.point_004: Potential complexity in managing multiple token IDs per token in decoder vocabulary. (Section 3 explains that multiple contextualized embeddings per token require assigning multiple token IDs, which may complicate decoding.)
critique_points.point_005: Lack of discussion on inference latency and efficiency compared to bi-encoder models. (While storage footprint is discussed, the paper does not provide detailed analysis of inference speed or computational overhead.)
critique_points.point_006: Unclear how well the approach generalizes to other retrieval tasks beyond document title retrieval. (Experiments focus on document retrieval with titles as targets; applicability to other retrieval formats is not explored.)
critique_points.point_007: The method relies on constrained beam search with prefix trees, which may limit flexibility. (Appendix A.1 describes constrained beam search to ensure generated sequences are in corpus, potentially restricting generation diversity.)
critique_points.point_008: How does CGR perform in terms of inference latency and computational cost compared to bi-encoder and vanilla generative retrieval models? (Understanding efficiency is critical for practical deployment of retrieval systems.)
critique_points.point_009: Can the approach be extended to retrieval tasks where targets are not fixed sequences like titles, e.g., passage retrieval or open-domain QA? (Evaluating generalizability would broaden the impact of the method.)
critique_points.point_010: How sensitive is the model performance to the choice of clustering parameter k and frequency of EMB updates in CGR Async? (Clarifying hyperparameter effects would help practitioners optimize the model.)
critique_points.point_011: Is it possible to jointly train EMB and RET end-to-end to improve coherency without incurring prohibitive computational costs? (Joint training might enhance embedding alignment and retrieval accuracy.)
